speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Nellie Wood Smith took place at her home in Bovill on January 23, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: When you first got to going back out to...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Is that your book?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, it is. Well, you got two of those. Oh yeah. I was going to say, did mine fall out of the...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It's in one of these boxes, or did I left it there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, that's mine. I was going to... You know how long you were going to be. I thought maybe I'd sit and take a little look at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you were saying you knew them from...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yes, well, her father was a drayman when they first moved on to Hiller. And those kids were little, man known.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so they... I don't know, I got to cry. And then they... Oh, another family lived there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the Millers were there for a while, weren't they? Who?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Millers were there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, there's the Lawrence's. And Lawrence's. And with this other family was the Smiths. A few years later, I see, he came out here out west and he had... And his brother had come on. And then the brother had worked a while and he sent the money back for his family to come on. And they came in and they settled there in Hiller. There was a house then, right... well, almost where the store stands down there. I think possibly a little bit further to the west. And that's where they lived. And then this other family, Grannis. Grannis' family lived there. I spoke to Mrs. Grannis in Lewiston recently. Well, she married Earl Grannis. But at that time, she was Kate Price.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so... But we got a Queen at the Mill. I went down to... we knew the... some of the Smiths' family. See, we had gotten a Queen to come down there and we knew them now. We had gone... oh, I don't know, several families. We knew everybody was there. And they used to have...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: oh, what is this?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They used to... like... get together meetings every evening. Sometimes they had kind of a study project and an entertainment project.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, in Hiller. We used to... it's kind of a thing. We'd have a literary society. I think that's what they called it, a literary society. We used to meet there every... I think every two weeks or something like that. Maybe every week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it in the school?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the schoolhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd come down from the homestead to that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that was more after we moved into town, I think. Because I don't think we ever... the only time we came down from the homestead and visited... just came down for a visit was while we were around those. I was about 14 or 15. But anyhow, Mother, you know, she was kind of... she's kind of like I am now, kind of shut in. Just couldn't go anywhere. We had no team or anything like that. And so finally she said to... she and Blanche Dinsmore, another cousin, was... Blanche was working out there at this big barn, where the big barn and the big house is. Where you turn right there after you come and leave Helmer. Turn to go across the railroad track. There's a large house there, you know, that sits... the trees have kind of shut it off and the roof... the view from the road now. But you'll notice that my cousin Blanche was working there for Uncle Henry, her uncle. And his wife had died. And she was working there. He had... before he'd go down to... the other Mrs. Dinsmore had lived down there by Troy. And so Blanche was kind of shut in too. She was there. He had some men working for him. And he took care of the place and the eats and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On their place there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: On Uncle Henry's place. That place then belonged to Uncle Henry. All that big meadow and that building, you know, and all. But there was just a log house there at that time. And... well, some... there was a barn and some things.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But... finally... Dad... he kept dead to build. He wanted to build a house. And so he... my dad built that big house that stands there. Now... they all called the... Well, they called it... the fellas that lived there. But it was really... shouldn't have gone by that name. But it did. But anyhow... It was arranged so that... well, they could get out for a little while. She hadn't seen any of the folks or visited anybody or anything for so long out there. And she got kind of tired. She got... I don't know, I guess through a letter or something. She and Blanche... that was just the girl that was working there. Uncle Henry. And they, I guess, probably through a letter, got a client out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why couldn't they send me out to help?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: There was a work. And she'd come in and visit their mother in a few days. And they'd have a good visit together. So... that's the way... she got on the horse. What they called her... oh, Dancer. They'd come in. One of them... I think that they... most of them... his name was supposed to be Dancer, but I think the old flaky guy, the man that did all the chores, kept up the wood and everything around there. He was Uncle Bill White, I think his name was. But anyhow, he called him... well, come on Lazarus, get going. I mean, he called him Lazarus. So she took the horse... She took the horse and went on up there to the horse. And I got the procedure to get on the horse and go back down. I wasn't much of a rider either, but I got there alright. And I knew just about what all was to do. I had stopped there, I think, once before because I got away once in a while. I was there for a day or for overnight or something when somebody was going out and I got a chance. But Mother didn't... she didn't want to send me out to then. We'd be there taking care of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She wanted you to stay home and help, you mean?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Now, she didn't like to leave me alone, you know, up in there to go out herself, you know, for a visit. But they arranged so they'd split up with me and change jobs. And I could take over that, but she figured I'd get along alright there. So I did and I was taking care of the pans and pans of milk and cream and the meals and things like that. Well, there was another lady there staying there too. Her husband was working for him and she was staying there too. She was helping. He had quite a lot of work to do. And she was staying there. I'd forgotten about her. Myrtle... she was Myrtle Compton was her maiden name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was there at the same time you were?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh. She was there when Blanche was there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how much work was there for you to do? Was it hard work?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, just a little bit more than... I guess maybe she could handle it alone, but I wasn't used to that much, you know. And I kept it going. The Myrtle and I kept it going. And so we had the milk to take care of the pans of milk and the buttery or pantry there. And skim it up and take care of the cream. And we churned all... it was one of these round barrel churns that you sit on the table and turn the wheel, you know. And it comes out the butter and buttermilk. And the mother just loved the buttermilk, but she wasn't there. But anyhow, Myrtle liked the buttermilk so well too. So, she said something about, oh boy, that's going to be a nice bunch of buttermilk to continue with. So we decided she should have some of that when she was churning.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And after we got it all turned into a big lump of butter, I said, well, here you want this buttermilk?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I said, yes, she's ready. I said, okay, I'll draw some for you. I put my glass down under the spout and pulled the plug and it squirted right out of my glasses. Squirt all of the flour in such a mess as we did have.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And both laughed and yet we had enough of us to clean up. She didn't have much buttermilk left, but they didn't miss it anyway. So sometimes I guess gave it to the pig, which is the new laughing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The buttermilk is what's left after you make the butter then, huh?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, that all comes out. My mother said she could just drink a gallon of it, she loved it. But it gets so much nicer and richer and different flavor than the buttermilk you buy from the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, that's, what should they call it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That's kind of a patent made, a fact made at the stores. And they say it's lacking some flavor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was buttermilk like? Did it taste like butter?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it has that rich buttery creamy flavor to it, kind of.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It looks awfully good, but I never did like sour milk of any kind. So my mother loved claver, claver milk. She'd take a bowl of it, you know, after, let it sour and get a big solid bowl. And then she'd just take a tablespoon and dip that out. And she loved the sour milk. Some people do like it. I never did stand the sour milk. Some of them eat sour milk, or claver with maybe salt and pepper on it. Some of them eat it with sugar. Put that down as sugar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is all that claver is, is just milk that's let stand to become real sour and then solid?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah. I see you're no farmer from way back. Well, I know, no, but I got to admit it, I'm not. Well, because if you let a glass of milk stand on your table or in the kitchen anywhere where it's kind of warm, and you leave that maybe until the next day or couple days, it'll turn to solid cake. Quite claver, you just slice it off. Just like butter, personally. And so that's, but you leave it standing like that until it gets claver. And then you put it down on the stove and cook it, and it comes out claver cheese, or cottage cheese. You see, it comes cooked a certain amount of time. It doesn't have to cook very long, but just so it's scalded through. And then put it in a strainer, a colander or something. And drain off the liquid of it, but it'd be just like water, to have the liquid distance off of it. And you've got the curd left for cottage cheese.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you don't have to cook it if you don't want to. You can just have claver milk by letting it stand. But if you want some cheese, just put the claver milk on to heat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the way people make cottage cheese?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. While we were living there on the homestead, we had a neighbor by the name of Fels. Mrs. Fels was a long, lean, lanky woman. And she had a short, fat husband. They were kind of an odd couple, you know. And she was German at the time. She didn't have any particular German brogue or anything like that. But she had just once or a while, you'd hear an expression, a German -like expression. And so, they just lived. I don't think it's more than a block. Just kind of round our little corners. You had to go to a gate out there. No, they lived closer to us. She just lived on the other side of the fence. Which is where they were then.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, uh...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, she had, they had a cow. They had a baby then about, oh I don't know, eight or nine months old, I think. And a little boy. So, anyhow, they kept this cow because they wanted to have milk for the baby. So then she had, well, milk, then she knew what to do with it, you know. Just him and her and the baby. So, she told mother, she said, well, anytime you want any milk or skim milk for anything, like, send a little kid over and she didn't have a lot of, she had lots of girl weight. And mother said, all right. So she sent a sister over there one day to get some milk. I think she was going to cook something she wanted milk for. And she sent her over there and they were just eating dinner and they got there. They were all up to the table and she asked her if she wouldn't set up a meal for them. And I said, oh no, she wasn't hungry. And she was kind of timid, you know. And especially when I was a stranger, she would, she would, Robert said no, she was, she was starting to fit in here. And I said, no, she didn't care for anything.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then they told ... and said, why? And they didn't give us, Miss G something, they said, do you, oh, do you like Smear case?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She said. And it didn't know what to say because she didn't know what the world's fair case was. So, and she didn't answer for long. She said something else about, we have some Smear case here.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And she said, would you like some of that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And Edna said, no, she didn't care for anything. She wasn't hungry or anything like that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And when she got home, she said, what in the world is Smear case?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She said, she looked around and the baby was just on the messy stage. And he ate his hands and he was smeared all over the face and both hands. She said, she looked around and she said Smear case. She asked if she liked Smear case. She didn't know what to say. She said she looked the only thing she could see was the baby. Was the worst Smear case of anything she saw.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She didn't know what the world she meant by Smear case. She didn't know how to tell her business. That was the cheese that she had made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I was talking to Floyd Lawrence, he said that the Phelps's were really, he was in bad health. And so they were very poor. They really had a hard time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They were quite, the baby should get out the ax and saw and work the way they did. And well, he was in poor health. He couldn't do much of anything. He's, I think he's so fat that it was crowding his heart a whole lot, I think. And it was a reason for his being careless about working much. And so he could just do a little bit and have to stop. He didn't get much done. But she'd go ahead and she was like a hook cracker, just tall and lanky. And she'd laugh and talk to them right on the head. Every day she was one of those good natured people.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And after they went to the homestead, they moved, the millers were here. And we would go up here and turn, and this was our place over here. And then, Felsus was on the fifth -grade, still friends. So when they were going to move up to their homestead then, they decided they called a house raising. And all the neighbors all around, if anything, went over there and put up the house. The log cabin.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The log cabin was kind of getting things ready beforehand. Put up the cabin and a barn, something like that. A wood shed or something like that. That's most of it. And you see, we lived, I don't know, six miles or eight miles. I can see that map up there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That's the courthouse I would see.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And we, you can pull that one out at the bottom there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We took our dinner. Everybody took their dinner with them and put that all in a bunch. And they had enough to feed the crew. Dinner there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hot coffee and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the house raising? Did you go over to that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: All the way up there. I think two miles further up to where we is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the whole neighborhood turn out for them?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, everybody around them. They had to pass the word around. Everybody got together, then they could throw it all up in a hurry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they do that for anybody? Or would they do it for them because they were in worse shape?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they knew that they were. They knew they needed help badly. But anybody that had been in the house like that, they thought. They were just people in there with nothing to do on or nothing to help with. You know, just making something out of nothing, you might say. And so they didn't pitch in and do anything for anybody. Just all get together and just get the day busy. And then they'd all talk and have a good visit while they were at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the women bring food from the different houses?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They all took the food, bachelors and all. They brought whatever they could, you know, and put it on the other, I don't know, makeshift table or something, I guess, that they had up there. They fixed it. I wonder if they had all those logs cut beforehand. I think they did a lot of it so that they could sort of prepare and do it together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if men went and helped them get those logs, too.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know. They might have. Of course, they didn't know much about the preparations. But I know they went up there. We all went and did what they could. And they were settled in their own home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems kind of rough to me that a family, let's say a man like he was not in good health, that they would go out and try to homestead.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It seems kind of tough. That was one way of, I guess, figuring that they would have a home that belonged to them, a piece of land of their own.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't think they knew that he was maybe as dead off as maybe as much as he was. He worked a while and he had to sit around rest a while. And eventually, after they moved into Global, he died suddenly. The dogs got, they had a dog, one of the neighbor's dogs, got into a site out there in front of the house. He just kind of started to go and separate them and suddenly got excited and just collapsed.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I had just been married just before that. She was, as soon as he had passed away, his name was Hampton Stelz, as soon as he had passed away, she just began to make plans to go back to Huntington, Indiana, where she came from. And she had the two youngsters by that time. It was a smear case.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then there was, oh I was going to say, I helped pack all of her dishes for her when she left and wrap them and pack them and all. And I bought some of her things for her. I bought a table. I was just starting housekeeping. I didn't have any furniture or anything. I didn't care, there didn't have to be something new and shiny. I just wanted something to use until when you're on, you got something to eat. A nice piece of food and we'd soon get stocked up with something no matter. So I bought a table and I think some chairs. And, oh, a couple of enormous big washtubs that were right brand new. Everybody washed with it. The original kind. She didn't... A boiler.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A boiler?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She didn't think of staying out here and trying to find another husband?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, she, that kid, except for her father, was still living back there. His name was...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Freehafer.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: original, her maiden name was Freehafer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Mr. Lawrence, Floyd Lawrence remembered her coming into the store once. And his father saying that he couldn't give him any more credit because, you know, he didn't have any money and he'd just been giving it to him for so long. She broke down and cried. She stood there and cried. So he broke down and gave it what she had to have. So it must have been real hard on him.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, there was no income, of course. They just didn't have even social security or anything like they have now, you see. They had no income. They just got set out there and they couldn't... Winter comes on, you see, then, because they couldn't raise anything. They had no chance making a garden right now and having it ready for the youth the next day. It takes quite a while to prepare a ground and raise a garden. So, anyhow, after they got moved up to the homestead and got going, she had... Well, she had... Her other baby there was about... He was a boy who was, I guess, maybe about a year old. And she'd walk into the homestead. You see, we were six miles out to our homestead, or possibly eight, whichever it was. And... They were two miles further. They were two miles further. That made... Well, eight miles, anyway, or possibly ten. And she'd strap that baby on her back, six feet pad, and she'd strike out for a hobo. She'd come in here and get her a bunch of groceries, and she'd be here by the time we had breakfast. We were living right over on the corner over there. You can see the spots for the folks that were living there. I wasn't married at that time. And when we first moved into town... We'd been here quite a while now, but they hadn't moved in yet. And she'd walk in there and carry that baby on her back, and stop at the house for a few minutes, and see how we all were, and how, well, I gotta get back. I got passed out of his dinner at a certain time and all. She'd go up to the store and get her groceries, a pack sack full of groceries. And then she'd start back, and we'd try to get her to eat her dinner before she left. She'd eat, but, no, I gotta get back to have her dinner. And she'd come in there and walk back that eight times a day, and take her dinner.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So it wasn't all fun. It was work to it as well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think she probably had to do a lot of the work that maybe he could have done if he was well?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yes, he'd been well. He could have done almost all of that work, but he just wasn't able to do it.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He'd work a little bit and then kind of play out. It was his heart that was bad. He just couldn't do it. She probably had to do all the chores, or both the chores. He couldn't even walk into our town. If he didn't have any arrows, he could only go a little ways. He'd have to sit on the rest of the pile. And I don't think he was going anywhere. I guess, I don't think he ever tried to come up. I think he looked there. I don't know whether they did. Maybe they did, but taking their time. I don't remember seeing him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she must have had stamina.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She was just like a piece of haywire. You could bend it and work with it until your fingers are raw and it still would be good. She was just tough as wire. And that's to do it. Go ahead and do it. And she had the... There, it's all right now.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And... The first time I saw her was when we moved to the homestead. We came along from the McGarry place. We were down at the Forks of Potlatch. We came up this way and we turned and went over a bridge and crossed the creek. Then we came up this side of the river, the creek you might say, and followed up this way.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We were going to our homestead that goes up, and when we got up here, the Feltses. His nephew, Mr. Felts' nephew, Ed Felts, and he had his son. I forgot what his name was, but he was about 14, 15 years old I guess, maybe 16. He was just a young kid. And, Basil. Basil Felts. And they lived here. And then you just go around, cross this, through this gate and through this fence. And Mr. Moore lived over here. And we lived up here, you see, actually. We just hadn't built our place yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you going to say the first time you saw her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh, the first time we saw her. And we got up there and we were coming along. Snow was kind of like it is now, it was nasty. And we were going in there, we had to be in a certain, not let a certain time lapse before you get back to that homestead again. You had a certain date to make or you're not going to lose your playing on it, you know. So we were going in to fulfill our agreement, however you call it. And my brother then was just a baby. I don't know about a year, year and a half old maybe. And we were going up in a sled. I don't know, some took us up there. I think maybe somebody from the mill, down the Denver mill, because they were relatives of ours. And we had been living there. Well, we were going to live there anyway, but just going up there for the time being I think. Just to spend a few days to establish residence. And so, even the filth was placed and the driver told us that's where the filth lived there. And about that time, the sled turned over. They had us kind of in a wide, flat bed, you know. And just a side wall I guess about that on it. And we had a batching outfit and stuff we could live in this extra place. There was millers and they had groceries and all the necessities if you could get by with it for about time. So, we had to be there. And the sled turned over and spilled it all out there in that snow. And he was a lady. It made him cry. And she came running up there. She just, they didn't live, oh I guess, maybe a little further from here to that woman's house, but from the road. And maybe as far as the second house. And she was there and that's the first time I met her. And she was tall, lean and lanky and just, well she just was nothing to her but skin and bones looked like, hardly. And then she started coughing and she'd cough and cough. I was scared. I told my mother the other day, I said, well that's my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He's me. I don't know what she's doing out in that snow. And they all did the same thing. And I looked for her to pass away anytime after we got there. My mom, she figured out the dog. She went back to, after she moved into town and he had been dying, she went back to Indiana and married another man. I don't know what they came over in the long run. I think she finally died back there but I don't know whether she outlived another man. She had married another woman before she married themselves. But that shows how, how the strength she had. She just looked like she'd be dead the next day or two. And she had that funny rattling cough. And it wasn't just a cold that just hung on her a few days. She only left. She just had a,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: what you call it, permanent cough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she came out, was every, was she helping you get the, get the sled to ride it again or what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I think the man that was taking us up there, he had straightened out. We had to get unloaded and get up and straighten ourselves up. And they tried to take everything for a short stay up there. And one thing, my mother had put in a box of matches for little kids. And they would come up there and just match us all over the snow. Still to match us all over. Anyhow, she wanted us to come on down to her place and come on in. And they said, no they didn't come on. We didn't have fun. They had to get settled in before night caught them and all. But it wasn't worth any of the hurt or anything. Just spilled out of the snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were living then at Miller's Extra House?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The ex -pats were going to stay all night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You hadn't built your own house?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No we hadn't. But was this house of his on your... No, it was just on his land. It's just up on the raise.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It might have been as far from here at that barn over there. That big vineyard block. It was up the hill. He had built his big house down by the creek. Then he would have that running water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that you didn't have a team at the house? Or a way of getting out?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, that was just a team from the mill. I think it's from the... Somebody from the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make it pretty rough for your family? Not being able to get around?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your father?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He worked and built a house and built a road and all like that. He came out here to build and got to work. He was a carpenter and over in the building business then. So he came out and went to work for the carpet and he would get back in each weekend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He built a house without horses?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the road too?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They brought in a load of lumber to him. I don't know whether Miller cut that lumber or not. He might have got lumber from Miller. They had a mill out there that I don't know how much it did or what kind of lumber it did cut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Building the road too? He did that without a team?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they had came for one day. They had you worked on the road. You got it built up until... Well, they came and come around again. We wanted to go all around. I had to cross a little creek and then go on up to the house. Well, we got to this creek. This man that we knew came in to visit the Millers. I don't know, spend the day here and maybe the night. And Dad asked how's their chances to let his team for the day. He had the stringers for the bridge that was going to cross this creek. He had the stringers in and all that. And he was going to put a corduroy bridge in on the little black pines there. And he was going to put that kind of top on it when he got around to it. But he had just gotten the stringers in so far and didn't have any more. So we got the team. He had plowed the side hills down so that we could work on them. We weren't getting leveled off into the road until we got to the bridge. And then Dad stopped us and the creek wasn't very full. It was only about that water about that far. But it was marshy and muddy and sandy and all. And Dad had plowed that down until he got to the bridge. Then he went to go eat lunch. He was going to take the team across there and tie them up on the other side. So we were going to eat lunch.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He took one horse across the water. And it was just a little stream with that much water. But anyhow, he gave me the reins for the other horse. And told me something. What he meant was for me, and I didn't understand him, was for me to hold that horse until he went over there and tied that other one up. And then he came back and did that. And that was a bit all right. I thought he took them on and he was going to get them on. I was kind of scared, but I didn't know. I had almost didn't think so. When I got a hold of that horse, well, honestly, I didn't come only about to his shoulders. They were those big kind of like that, what you see on the TV, the big deer signs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I know. I'm not sure what kind of a person is in the perch.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, kind of that kind of thing. It's a log you see with those enormous big horses here. And that's about the kind of slant hand. Great big, enormous things. And their feet, old great big feet, you know, like that. And anyhow, I kind of went until Dad got out of the way. And I started as my horse, but I got a hold of the reins that clip under his right, you know, of the harness somewhere. So I did kind of, I figured kind of hold him better if I had to put a short rein on him or then the kite, he couldn't wander around much. So, and by me having hold of there, and he was, oh, that much higher, he's had, you know, way up higher than I was. And he, of course, is, of course, doing it working for himself, doing the best that he knew what he was doing, and I didn't. So he started lunging and, you know, hurrying to get like a horse wheel. He'd get over that wet spot before he was kind of mired on, you know, each step he'd take. He'd go down, but he'd get a, he'd get out of that. It made sense. But when he was doing that, stepping here and there, I just made a foot cracker out of me. I didn't, I couldn't control him at all. But I was hanging on, and then he stepped on this way, you know, I stepped, and how I, he had the, I had to step that way to catch him. He caught me with my feet crossed when I stepped back, something. And anyhow, when he stepped back in on the other side, to the right, I just smashed that foot right down into the grass and into the sand and gravel and mud.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I held him dead and came and I said, Well, why in the world did you start this happy?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I told you to wait for me. And I said, Well, I thought you said thank you. He said, I wasn't coming back after him. And I had a nice black and blue foot for quite a while. And I guess it injured some of them. They didn't get to fight over it entirely, so it's been a weekend. It's been pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It would have been a hurt a lot worse if you hadn't sunk into the mud.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, up in the mud and sand and stuff. I would have just crushed a lot of bones in it, if I hadn't been. But it just went into the mud.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father mad at you? Was he worried about you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, both. I think, yes, he was kind of, he wasn't exactly not disgusted, because he told me to leave now and come back and get everything. I thought he said, Bring him home.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then he plowed that afternoon, the hillside, he plowed that down. That is pretty near to our house, the bridge was. Maybe as far as from here, maybe a block. So he got that all plowed down so he could use a shovel and get it all leveled up.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So that's all the work that was done with horses and our roads. We just took it to hand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he wanted supplies or groceries, something he had to have, would he walk into Bovill or into Helmer to get it?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We always got our groceries at Helmer. And of course we lived at Helmer, we lived there at the homestead quite a while before he got to coming to Bovill to work, just living there and working on the place. But he would somehow go into something, but we didn't go to clear into Helmer or something. We'd go as far as Miller's Place, you see, that was only, oh I don't know, from one to two miles, I don't know. It might have been an hour and a half or something like that, but it wasn't so very far. And they'd go that far and leave their notes, whatever it was that they wanted. And the fellow Hayes at the roadways, we'd turn to it this way, our homestead, but he had done up straight on ahead. So he lived in his want list, and he never had a whole list of things to bring out for everybody. Whenever he got back, he knew just about what they were doing, and they'd take their list down ahead of time, I'll be sure. And he'd have it there when he got ready to go in town. And he'd bring back a load of groceries for everybody. And we'd go and get ours for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father would pack it back to the homestead?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Because after we got our little doll finished, and our house finished and all, some of the folks from the mine, or from the mill, came up to see us. Once or something like that. As long as you could drive up there. I think they were up there one time. I know they came up to Mobile on their China anniversary. They'd come to get their anniversary. The folks came up from Troy. They got some subtle raves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your parents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The road, was there a main road any way up there? Or did you have to build the county road yourself?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, that has been built by, I don't know who built it, but you see the McGarry lived on, well it was a kind of a commentary thing, it was kind of a cliff thing stuck out over where they built the road down below. But the McGarry had a house right up on the top of that thing. And it was up at the Forks of Potlatch down there. They went in from Helmand, right across the road. Turned at that corner, I mean they went straight from Helmand, but that's where we turned to come to Global, where they went straight out and across the railroad, in a short distance after you leave here. Stayed highway here to Global. But anyhow, the McGarry lived up on that, commentary, that stuff out there, and you cross the bridge, and when you come over here, Belts and Miller, they both been living there, and they had teams, and I don't know how anymore, if it was any of the best of them had teams up there, but there was so many of them, and you could go clear on up to Hazier, that was about,two miles I think from that place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what you had to do was pretty much put in just off from the main road to your...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We had to bench off of Miller and Hazes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And get into your place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say, how far do you think that was? Quarter mile? Half a mile?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, that was, it wasn't that, oh maybe a quarter mile from Miller's place, you're up to where we turned. And then we had about, well a mile and a half I guess, you see it's about two miles I think, a mile and a half to two miles, from the Miller place to our place. I don't know, I could tell by the map, but I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just trying to guess at how much road your father had to put in on your place.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well it was, it had all, it was, I don't know, it was a mile and a half maybe to our place, or whether it was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was quite a bit though. Sounds like quite a bit of road he had to put in.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was a dense forest, you know, or what did you want to say, native, this is who it was, you know. And, but that was, oh I don't know, it was less than two miles from our place to Miller's, but it was only about a quarter of a mile, or half a mile maybe, up to where we turned off the Miller's road. And then they built the rest of it, you see. And then ours became a lead road to Miller's, I mean to Rookses. They had a route where they lived in our place, you know. We turned to the right and went down through the canyon and on over another half mile or so, maybe a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Rookses built it from your place?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh, they lived in from, not from our place, but from up on the hill. Just a little file for you, and then we got to, you know, just before we turned to get, went down to that bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just before you went down the creek?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And they kept going straight. And they crossed the creek, or they kept there. They went on down the hill, kind of. And that's where they went down, that's where they later put in camp, oh camp six. They put a camp in there and loaded and hauled the timber out there for a long time. And where they were, their homestead, they used, see they had part of theirs made on ours and they didn't have much to make. Then they would, they just went right up the hill, back to their place, and they would come out there to our house. They just on foot, you know. They'd come up there, they'd let us come out. He had his old man and his two sons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Brookses?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father do the building all by himself on that road, or did the family help him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, we were all men on that road.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But, I, my brother was just an old one, a year too old, just a little fell.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And most of the time, well maybe mother would stay home and make up a lot of stuff and get ready for several days run, you know. So maybe the next day he had something to do, maybe he wanted some of her help. So maybe something they thought I couldn't do, maybe something like that. So she'd leave me with the baby and take care of him. And she'd go to bed, maybe had enough to go along, or maybe not, and she'd maybe be home with the baby, but maybe not. But, and then other days when we'd all go. And work. And he takes a little two -wheeled cart, and they could pull him in, and bring him along over. So then he'd ride along and they'd have to maybe cheat and became the baby. All day.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And anyway, in the long run, she was, oh he was, I don't know, just playing or sleeping, or some sleeping baby or something. And she had little hatches and she was chopping the switches, you know, and little pieces. And she just chopped into my finger, putting it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Edna?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh. And I just patched that up right at the way it creaked. She said, all right, she wouldn't know anything happened. She didn't chop any more wood after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were you doing that too? Did you chop out the brush?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, because Mother and I, and we'd help in salt logs, you know, the first one of us on the other week, going through the heavy labor. We'll just take care of the baby.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, you know, they just, well, just made it brush, and just take all the, everybody had the idea, mapped out for everyone on the road, so we'd just take that all out. And the hatches there, take out the brush, and saw the logs, and put it all away. And use it, you know, to stick on it, to tie the hole, I guess it was, and use it for candles. And roll the logs there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work hard on that, or did you work sort of leisurely?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, we kept busy.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: We were anxious to get it finished up, so we could get into it, you know. And we started building the house. Dad brought in a load of lumber from somewhere. I don't know where he got that, but he got it from the mill down at Chai. The folks, our folks, you know, run that mill, and they might have gotten the lumber from them. Or, probably he got it from Miller's, where they had the sawmill right there close. But anyway, he brought in a load of lumber one time, and then, of course, he could make all the studying and the things out of the little timber there. If he wanted to, if he had to. So, I don't remember whether he used that for all, or whether he used two before, it's wherever he had to. But I know that he wasn't thinking anything about that then. He didn't, but Mother helped him with a lot of that. And she carried shingles and those, to put the rough on. And she would hand them up to him, and he'd nail them all in his chest, and she'd bring them up.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is this?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh, he didn't have those shingles at first. He made those shingles out of cedar, right down there by that little creek where the horse stepped on me. As far as, he had our shingle factory, and he made all those shingles by hand. And then he took his drawer knife and shaded them so they were thin edge, and you know, widened at the other end, like they had been thin edge, just like the factory shingle.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I didn't make so many of those shingles. He didn't trust me too much with that drawer knife, but I did some of it. But then when we started to build our barn, we took poles, you know, we had lots of, there was an awful lot of little black pines, those tall, little tiny little pines that just grew clear up and over without the tapering. They didn't come up to anything. And we had a nice barn, a big barn there. So, and I know I made shakes for the barn, and for the chicken house. Made a little chicken house out of gloves. And I made a lot of the shingles, the shakes and all that. I could make the shakes alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The shakes, you mean using a fro?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, it didn't make it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Use your, what do they call that thing now?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: If you split those shingles, it's a blade. With a handle that runs up this way. And you sit it and use your mallet on that blade. I forgot what they call it, but I made quite a few of those shingles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that called a cedar fro?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I thought I heard it called a fro. It's a fro.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well now, did he start doing the buildings, building the buildings? He did that before he got the road in, didn't he?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, he couldn't. Put the road in first. I don't know, he might have started, but he couldn't do much, because he couldn't live there. You know, until he got the house built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were still staying at Miller's house?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, up in the little house up in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was really after you got the road in that he brought in the lumber and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long do you think you folks worked down the road before you started building?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it most of the season?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, not a lot. It was every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start in the spring?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, I had to stop and think, I was like, how am I going to tell? That's what I was thinking, you started in the spring, and then when did you start on the house?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: we started because I left Troy, left school, a month I think, before school was out. I think it was May day that I... It was some special day.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It was Easter, but no, I think it was May day, but then the next day after we left to go to the homestead, and I was worried because I was afraid I'd lose that year's credits at school. And he said, no, I said, I know. I'm getting credit for that because he said that work was easy for me. He said, you know, I'll have to do that without any more. He said, you complete the rest of that year's work without any more instruction or any more study.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then did you start, do you remember about when you started doing the house? Was it summer?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh yes, we had to, you see, we started right in on the road, from Miller's Place, the branch house. We started in and worked on that every day, as long as it would be like the last meal. We always took a lunch with us and worked on the road until we got it passable.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It's just that it's more money, so we worked on the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he grade, did he use the horse and grade the whole length of the road when he had it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he easy to work with?

speaker: Was he easy to work with?
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because I know a lot of times it's pretty rough on a, let's say for, you know, a man and his wife to work together, because maybe he's bushy and he gives too many orders or, you know, that kind of thing. Sometimes they don't... I don't think they'll remember this. Yeah.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And after we started working all day, he did, maybe, I don't know whether it was there that day or something. I know he used to tell me, bring me a so -and -so, whatever, and he'd be out of his toolbox or some of his tools, you know, and he'd tell me, bring me this or that or a certain chisel or a certain bevel or a certain, you know, plane or, well, I could go and pick out that, I knew just what he was talking about, every tool in the country, in the thing, I guess. So I worked with him enough that I knew the tool's the heart. And I liked it. I liked the present work. I enjoyed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can't imagine that there were too many girls who were, had the chance to do as much work as you did in that, you know, building up or pioneering.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, there really wasn't, because the Millers, the Millers and the Felts, both the Felts and the Hayes and all of our neighbors, Aunt McGarry's, all of them, none of them had any larger children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they had young kids?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, Felts's had the baby and Almevitz had two little kids, that's after we got the house all done though. She lived with us quite a while. Her husband was working at the mine. And they were working at the mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She lived with your family?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She had two kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really? Did they have their house up yet?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They hadn't built it yet?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know whether they built a house later or whether... I don't know how. I know they were living there because I guess they didn't have any place yet to live. We just didn't know each other. We had to live with the next fellow until he got him a place fixed for his own. I guess that was... I don't know how. But the Hollandaix, we didn't know that in the East, but we knew the people that did know them and they were all friends together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she stayed right at the house with you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She worried Mother to death but near with us. She said, take those kids. She had one little girl, I think about three, and a boy about five. And he had a little terror. Either one of them was bad enough. Mother was particular about her begging things. It had to be kept clean. He didn't tramp around on over with your shoes and things like that. You just had little manners if you were out in the sticks. You're supposed to hate yourself. And so she laid down... We didn't have enough beds so they laid the feather bed off. Mother had a feather bed and they laid that off on the floor and then fixed the bed for her and the two kids on that feather bed. But when Frankie would come in to the house from outside and all, he'd just go clompity -clomp with his old dirty shoes right across the bed and all and that annoyed Mother. And she'd have a fuss with her and just annoyed her death. I think that was responsible for her. She said she thought that's what did it for her, having an Alpha 6 bill that she couldn't have passed away on us out there. She miscarried on us out there 40 miles from nowhere. And so I think that's the feeling. Finally, there was Holland that got things straightened around and we'd sent for the extra. That's the time that she wouldn't let me go through the woods alone just to get the mail. I could take the 22 and go over to Miller's and get the mail and come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this? Your mother didn't want you to go through the woods?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, she didn't go over there to get the mail but for anything else. And Ora Hayes was about the only one. He was the one that lived on a little further. He was our little giant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? She was having a miscarriage and what happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, she was just so on edge and so worried and so nervous. She was a doctor. That was the only thing I did with this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get the doctor? What happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She wrote a note. Dad was in to Moscow at that time to prove up on the homestead and could bring to her what is so -and -timber. I think that's what was the occasion for him to be out that way. He had gone out to Troy and was supposed to be back that day. And she was just something that just wouldn't have lasted much longer. I don't think she just would have invited us. Anyhow,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: she had gotten up and got... She did know enough to lift the foot of the bed and put a mother, I guess, a new teller to, and put puffs or a chunk of wood under the legs of the foot of the bed so as to put her head down and all and stop that hemorrhaging. So she told me, asked if I could go to Miller's and take this note for him to bed. She wrote a note and I had said that. She told him that she wished she'd hurry home. She said, I haven't sent Ory Hayes on down to get the doctor from Troy. And he said, I don't think I can last much longer at this rate, she said.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Down there, the other side of Helmer, there's a place... That little house that lives over there where they used to have a...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Another little... A place where you could sleep all night and things, you know. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you thinking of the halfway house, kind of, or was it just different?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Lawrence's had a halfway house and so did the Wells's. No, it was on Dunfridger. No, not as far as Wells's. It's right there now, right on the middle there. And you've noticed that place, it has a little trailer house on your right as you're coming this way. Well, that is the... I don't know what else they call it now, but that house... I don't know if I can think of it. But that was the place for... Now, they didn't used to go past those houses there. It went on the other side of that and then it went up a steep hill and then you get on over to where you'd go on and over to our place. But that was long ways from the other side.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But anyhow... I don't know how she said so, but don't go any further and all. I said, well, I'm gonna be going up to Ora's. Ora Eastley always called me Ora Hayes. And I said, I can make it. I'm not afraid. And she told me to take the gunner's, 22. And she said, take over the millers. Find some of them and they'll go and tell Hayes. I went over there and I don't know if there was anybody home or there wasn't anything I could... And I just... That's the first time I really, really disobeyed that I remember. But she told me not to go there. She's afraid that I could... I'd get lost in the woods or something. I scooted out of there fast, as fast as I could go up to Ora's and took in that note. And he said, okay. He said, I'll be right over here. Jumped on his saddle horse and took off. And when he went up on that hill part, just went down to Harrekin. He had to go through that awful road and it's muddy now, going down that hill. Or people that walk would go up that hill and climb it because of a shortcut. Well, they were so much, I'd rather walk clear around that way. And he thought, well, his horse could make it that much faster too. So he said, yes he'd go and take the shortcut. And he took that shortcut and met Dad all night. Just lucky that he went that way because it was meant to be that way.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Hi.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Go ahead.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And did you get your horse? Tender too?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It cost me $30 to get a horse.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did you have one of them yet?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh huh. Okay. So, so. And he gave Dad his note and then he just flew right up. Just jumped on that horse and flew down. So then went on to the Dry Creek mill. That was below Deary.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You know where that used to be?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that was the other cousins, who owned that mill too. They were running it. And they had a phone. And he called into Troy to get the doctor to get out there. Or, well, I guess he called the other mill down there by Troy and had them get the doctor and bring him up there. And so they settled up and got him. And it was up, I don't know, I think it was up in the night, right at the night before they got there. But Dad also went on to Mother.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And so she came out of it alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did your father go all the way to the Dry Creek himself?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh no, he just went home.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Karen knows that he just practically runs most of the way home. He's so worried about nothing. He went on his horse. And of course the doctor knew what to do and all. He, I guess, knew what he was going for and so he took all his materials and packed them in the room.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: He had a hammer on him. He had a few things on him. But I don't think he was alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well that sounds just so, you know, so close to being tragic.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But that turned out to have been very, if it had much orthopedic on this other way, he missed him. Well, that wouldn't have been, of course Dad might not have been already tired from that hike. But he might not have come right on. He might have stopped and carried some factory others. And he got that note. He didn't stop for anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you must have been all pretty frightened.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: You were. You just.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You too, I imagine. I know I would have been.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was, well I was, I was maybe fourteen at that time. But, I don't know about it, I had two good pals. I'm not sure how it would have been. Of course this is stuff that, well I may not be putting all that on if we get the machine trained on but. It's stuff that.

words: Probably a big strain on her. Uh huh.

words: She said she. I think this is. Frankie was such an honorable lesson. You could. His mother. Not Frankie. She'd have it right. And do it. You know this. And Frankie did that. And Frankie didn't hear it at all.

words: Do you think that not having a horse made it much harder on the family?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It would seem to me that if you had a horse and so you could have gotten around more quick, quicker, it would have been easier for you.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I don't know. I couldn't have taken that horse and ridden down there like he did. He had a riding horse, you know, he just jumped on there and take off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let any grass grow under his feet. Like for your father, if he had to, you know, he would have to walk and take a mile longer to get in.

words: Yeah. He didn't. We had to call while we were out there.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Found another cow for the mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me when we were talking at the beginning here about staying down at. That's right. His place while she came up and visited with your mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you're telling me, you know, we're talking about that for a while, but did you finish that story?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you were down there and for, that you said was like one of the first times that you were right by Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, that was over there down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get a chance to visit around in town when you were down there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, there was no way of us getting, we just had to stay and do the work and cook and tend to things.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So, although I believe, I have to say one time, it must have been that they had that little party at their house. And no, I'm glad she was there at the party too. Those neighbors right around close to the house down there at the corner, the house dead, you know. There was a teacher who was boarded with them. Teaching school there. This, I can't think of her name now, but a young girl. And she invited all the neighbors all around and said a word to them. And they'd come over and have a party and get together. So we had a party and it was all had on the grown ups, a whole bunch of long -legged boys

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: and I guess it was a few girls, but mostly boys that were there. And we'd all sit, big green around on the floor and play games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did you ever play circus?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they used flinch cards and they dealt out until the deck's all gone. So they'd pile, you know, to take them to the country. And then when a certain sign was given, everybody turned the card. And if your card matches, the first thing though, before they do that, after they get their cards all distributed face down, finally they choose the name of an animal that they would be at the circus. And when you flip your card over, if your card matches somebody else's, you call their name before they call yours and you get to give them your cards. And the idea is to give away your cards as quick as possible. One can get rid of his card and beat. And they get so tangled up, the time you get a dozen on here, all of them, same cat, dog, mouse and elephant and everything else. And they couldn't think of the right name for the right person. The craziest thing is if you had more fun, you'd laugh to the risk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd just more fun and different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to shout out the name of their animal?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Different. And you'd call everybody else's maybe instead of that one. But that's funny even yet. That sounds pretty funny. It is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was this party while you were working at the guy's place?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think that's when I was down there. I don't remember going down any special time.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: But it might have been that I'd have stayed all night there sometime when I was coming back from maybe Centralia. And I'd have stayed all night to meet the folks going back in home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go down to visit a Troy occasionally?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I did once in a while. And I rode up with the old mail carrier. He was an old fellow, about 75 I guess or so. And then he'd stop at the halfway house up there, the Derry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wellses?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it was right close to Wellses. Wells had one, but Stockville's had one right there close. And we knew Stockville was real well. So they, that though I believe had, I think that was after we moved up here. Because I didn't know the Stockville's before. And I moved out to the homestead. It must have been better now. They'd stop there and eat dinner. At the Stockville's. Of course, this Stockville's a wonderful cook. She had, I think, eight or nine kids. I don't know the heck of us. Most of us all grown up except, well, you saw in the paper the other day where this Stockville's in Idaho. This Stockville's house burned. She had married the youngest Stockville boy, Carlton. And I went to school with all those kids. It was Maddie and Clara, Carlton and Jim. And Bess, Bessie. And then the rest of them were grown up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Lou Wells?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Uh -huh. Just to speak to her. I just didn't get a chance to see them very often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Agnes Leener? Did you know her?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She was the one who got shot. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that happening at the time? When that happened?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, it was down at the... I was thinking of that the other day. Under the name of that mine. Mizpaw. Mizpaw Mine. I couldn't figure it. And she was flunking down there. Hashing with the... I guess for the miners, for the fellas working there. And this other kid, this Dittleman, was... I don't know, I don't know. He was working at the regular worker. He was getting wood and helping with the other pot. But anyway, he got her to go for a walk with him. And they walked down to one of these carons on the tunnel. And he enticed her back into that and insisted on a rape deal. And she fought him. And they were fighting it out and he shot her.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: So... Had a pretty little back there where she couldn't be heard helping for helping, I guess.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I was just wondering about it the other day. I wonder whatever became of him. I don't remember whether he got sentenced.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard a couple of different stories. I heard that he shot himself in the mine. And then I also heard that he got out.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: That he was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That he shot himself and didn't die. I heard he shot himself and did die. And I also heard he got sentenced. But I don't remember how that... How it turned out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know her?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes, I knew her well and I knew him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have thought that he would do something like that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well... I wouldn't put him past him. Maybe if he had a chance to get her off in the place. But I don't think he thought he'd come to that... That final time. I think he thought he had his own way. And when he comes to that, I don't think that... I guess he had that gun in case... Maybe he carried it all the time. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he was married, wasn't he?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't think so. Oh, I don't know. Maybe he wasn't. I don't think so. And then went on for years after that. And I know... Hey, it was my two. My dad just... After we moved into town. Dad used to play for the dancers up here sometimes. He played the violin. And the mother and the folks, we all went to the dance that night.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And then he went to walk me home. And he wanted to know if he could bring me home. And I didn't know how it was. I kind of remembered, you know, something about being cut about since that had happened. No, that was... I don't know either now, but that is because I heard he didn't have a big reputation. Maybe that hadn't happened before. But I kind of doubted him with a very good reputation. And so I didn't know how to say anything about him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wasn't going with anybody, wasn't I?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Excuse me, just a minute.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I'm asking you to... Ask if you could take me home to the dance, you know. This was Dillman. Jess Dillman. Uh -huh. And I... This might have been... I guess it was maybe before this other thing happened. Because I bet I had thought the people didn't speak very well of him, so... I didn't know whether to say yes or no. And I said, well, just a moment. I'll see what Mother says about it or something like that. And so I thought that would be one way of getting him out of my hair. And so, anyhow, I told Mother that he wanted to take me home and walk me home.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I said, what should I tell you?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She said, well, oh, I don't know, I guess it wouldn't hurt anything. As long as you walk her along home with the rest of us, she said, you know, with the whole family. Well, I guess it wouldn't hurt anything. So I did, and I said, no. She said, it'll be all right. I said, yes. I said, we'll walk her over to the rest of the room. So he never did ask me for any more appointments. I think he didn't like the idea of being in the whole family. He thought he'd get me off to one side, maybe. But I just wasn't about to wait.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And I'm sure now it must have been before this other happened. Because I don't think I knew that at the time or when this happened. Oh, no, I don't imagine he was still around the country after that happened. Yes, that's right.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't know. I didn't feel that upon the...

words: But I thought, well, he'll find out anyhow that if he's discriminated with that kind of crowd, he's got to be a gentleman. And anyhow, if he isn't, he won't be about it anymore.
